content:
I graduated as a master student with distinction from the University of Birmingham at Computer Science. I am very fortunate to have been supervised by Dr. Peter Hancox for my thesis on Fine-grained Sentiment Analysis of Yelp-review and Exploring Multi-class Imbalanced Data.
When I am not working. I like to spend time exploring the city and listening to a Chinese singer. And I run a database query website for this singer, used by a fan group of around 40 people. This website involves around 160,000 data, developed with MySQL database, Python crawler technology, and Flask framework.
summary:
I graduated as a master student with distinction from the University of Birmingham at Computer Science. I am very fortunate to have been supervised by Dr. Peter Hancox for my thesis on Fine-grained Sentiment Analysis of Yelp-review and Exploring Multi-class Imbalanced Data.
When I am not working. I like to spend time exploring the city and listening to a Chinese singer. And I run a database query website for this singer, used by a fan group of around 40 people.
title: Yuzhen Li

content:
Flexibility This feature can be used for publishing content such as:
 Online courses Project or software documentation Tutorials  The courses folder may be renamed. For example, we can rename it to docs for software/project documentation or tutorials for creating an online course.
Delete tutorials To remove these pages, delete the courses folder and see below to delete the associated menu link.
Update site menu After renaming or deleting the courses folder, you may wish to update any [[main]] menu links to it by editing your menu configuration at config/_default/menus.toml.
For example, if you delete this folder, you can remove the following from your menu configuration:
[[main]] name = "Courses" url = "courses/" weight = 50  Or, if you are creating a software documentation site, you can rename the courses folder to docs and update the associated Courses menu configuration to:
[[main]] name = "Docs" url = "docs/" weight = 50  Update the docs menu If you use the docs layout, note that the name of the menu in the front matter should be in the form [menu.X] where X is the folder name. Hence, if you rename the courses/example/ folder, you should also rename the menu definitions in the front matter of files within courses/example/ from [menu.example] to [menu.<NewFolderName>].
summary: Learn how to use Academic's docs layout for publishing online courses, software documentation, and tutorials.
title: Overview

title: Example Page 2

content:
Click on the Slides button above to view the built-in slides feature.   Slides can be added in a few ways:
 Create slides using Academic's Slides feature and link using slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file Upload an existing slide deck to static/ and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file Embed your slides (e.g. Google Slides) or presentation video on this page using shortcodes.  Further talk details can easily be added to this page using Markdown and $\rm \LaTeX$ math code.
summary: An example talk using Academic's Markdown slides feature.
title: Example Talk

content:
In traditional coursework, CS students are taught Data Structures, Algorithms, Operating Systems, Machine Learning, etc. However, there is no specific course on the tools that must be used to put this knowledge into practice. For example, once you get into Linux, you will definitely use the Shell, and if you want to track changes to source code, you can't get around git. Most students know about these tools through their classmates or teachers or by reading some blogs online. The result of this is that knowledge of the tools is very shallow and scattered.
MIT 6.null The Missing Semester of Your CS Education designed to help computer science students fill in gaps in their education that may have been missed in traditional coursework and build a systematic understanding of CS pratical tools. The course is taught by experienced instructors and includes video lectures, readings, assignments, and programming projects. And the course materials are available online, and students can work through the material at their own speed.
Personally, most of the content of the course has already been used in my work so I've had some practice, but I'm still hoping to review and develop systematic knowledge through this open course.
Overall, MIT 6.null The Missing Semester of Your CS Education is an excellent resource for computer science students who are looking to fill in gaps in their education and develop practical skills that will help them in their careers. Whether you are a current student or a professional looking to expand your knowledge, this course is well worth exploring. With its emphasis on practical applications and flexible, self-paced learning, it offers a unique and valuable learning experience for anyone interested in computer science.
The following output is based on the Tencent could server
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ cat /etc/redhat-release CentOS Linux release 7.6.1810 (Core)  NOTES Core of Shells: allow you to run programs, give them input, and inspect their output in a semi-structured way.
The shell parses the command by splitting it by whitespace, and then runs the program indicated by the first word, supplying each subsequent word as an argument that the program can access.
ls command: To see what lives in a given directory.
Some other handy programs to know about at this point are mv (to rename/move a file), cp (to copy a file), and mkdir (to make a new directory).
man command: It takes as an argument the name of a program, and shows you its manual page.
In the shell, programs have two primary “streams” associated with them: their input stream and their output stream.
< file and > file: Let you rewire the input and output streams of a program to a file respectively.
| operator: lets you “chain” programs such that the output of one is the input of another.
sudo command: It lets you “do” something “as su” (short for “super user”, or “root”).
EXERCISES  For this course, you need to be using a Unix shell like Bash or ZSH. If you are on Linux or macOS, you don’t have to do anything special. If you are on Windows, you need to make sure you are not running cmd.exe or PowerShell; you can use Windows Subsystem for Linux or a Linux virtual machine to use Unix-style command-line tools. To make sure you’re running an appropriate shell, you can try the command echo $SHELL. If it says something like /bin/bash or /usr/bin/zsh, that means you’re running the right program.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ echo $SHELL /bin/bash  Create a new directory called missing under /tmp.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ mkdir /tmp/missing/ [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos tmp]$ ls -l total 8 drwxrwxr-x 2 yuzhencode yuzhencode 4096 Feb 17 04:59 missing  Look up the touch program. The man program is your friend.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ man chmod  Use touch to create a new file called semester in missing.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ touch semester [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l total 0 -rw-rw-r-- 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 0 Feb 17 05:05 semester  Write the following into that file, one line at a time:  #!/bin/sh curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu  The first line might be tricky to get working. It’s helpful to know that # starts a comment in Bash, and ! has a special meaning even within double-quoted (") strings. Bash treats single-quoted strings (') differently: they will do the trick in this case. See the Bash quoting manual page for more information.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ echo '#!/bin/sh' > semester [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat semester #!/bin/sh [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ echo 'curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu' >> semester [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat semester #!/bin/sh curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu  Try to execute the file, i.e. type the path to the script (./semester) into your shell and press enter. Understand why it doesn’t work by consulting the output of ls (hint: look at the permission bits of the file).  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester -bash: ./semester: Permission denied [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l total 4 -rw-rw-r-- 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 31 Feb 17 06:38 semester  Run the command by explicitly starting the sh interpreter, and giving it the file semester as the first argument, i.e. sh semester. Why does this work, while ./semester didn’t?  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ sh semester HTTP/1.1 200 OK Connection: keep-alive Content-Length: 7987 Server: GitHub.com Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * ETag: "63e8ee3f-1f33" expires: Thu, 16 Feb 2023 15:14:55 GMT Cache-Control: max-age=600 x-proxy-cache: MISS X-GitHub-Request-Id: BD92:11EC:CC0C5:1144EC:63EE4617 Accept-Ranges: bytes Date: Sun, 17 Feb 2023 22:44:25 GMT Via: 1.1 varnish Age: 112 X-Served-By: cache-nrt-rjtf7700060-NRT X-Cache: HIT X-Cache-Hits: 1 X-Timer: S1676846665.485254,VS0,VE1 Vary: Accept-Encoding X-Fastly-Request-ID: 224acc2983ac63337758858b6d8a36f3ada14784  Look up the chmod program (e.g. use man chmod).  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ man chmod  Use chmod to make it possible to run the command ./semester rather than having to type sh semester. How does your shell know that the file is supposed to be interpreted using sh? See this page on the shebang line for more information.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ chmod +x semester [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l total 4 -rwxrwxr-x 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 31 Feb 20 06:38 semester [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester HTTP/1.1 200 OK Connection: keep-alive Content-Length: 7987 Server: GitHub.com Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * ETag: "63e8ee3f-1f33" expires: Thu, 16 Feb 2023 15:14:55 GMT Cache-Control: max-age=600 x-proxy-cache: MISS X-GitHub-Request-Id: BD92:11EC:CC0C5:1144EC:63EE4617 Accept-Ranges: bytes Date: Sun, 17 Feb 2023 22:42:33 GMT Via: 1.1 varnish Age: 0 X-Served-By: cache-nrt-rjtf7700073-NRT X-Cache: HIT X-Cache-Hits: 1 X-Timer: S1676846554.748478,VS0,VE222 Vary: Accept-Encoding X-Fastly-Request-ID: fa9c19ae05a08a1a94ddd4ef81131b157124d936  Use | and > to write the “last modified” date output by semester into a file called last-modified.txt in your home directory.  [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester | grep --ignore-case last-modified > ~/last-modified.txt [yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat ~/last-modified.txt Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT  Write a command that reads out your laptop battery’s power level or your desktop machine’s CPU temperature from /sys. Note: if you’re a macOS user, your OS doesn’t have sysfs, so you can skip this exercise.  C:\WINDOWS\system32>wmic /namespace:\\root\wmi PATH MSAcpi_ThermalZoneTemperature get CurrentTemperature CurrentTemperature 3212
summary: MIT 6.null missing-semester notes and exercises solutions.
title: MIT 6.null-00 Course Overview and the Shell

content:
Programming is like a magic tool for me, I like to use it to solve all kinds of problems. I can always find something fun and learn something new from it.
 This blog talks about the general solution for k-Sum problem. From 2Sum to 2+1Sum, then 2+1+1Sum. Utimately, the generic solution came out. k-Sum Problem is like Input n integers (positive and negative) and Decide are there k numbers in the input that sum to target integer?
Speaking From 2Sum Problem which is the most basic problem.
1. 2Sum  Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.
  Example 1: Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 Output: [0,1] Explanation: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].
 1） Brute Force Solution It is obvious that two nested loops can exhaust all sums.
def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]: for i in range(0, len(nums)): for j in range(i + 1, len(nums)): if nums[j] == target - nums[i]: return [i, j]  Time complexity: $$O(n^2)$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$
2） Use the Hashtable or Hashmap Feature of the Programming Language def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]: map = {} for i, n in enumerate(nums): m = target - n if m in map: return [map[m], i] else: map[n] = i  Time complexity: $$O(n)$$ Space complexity: $$O(n)$$
3） Two Pointers Technique def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]: nums_sorted = sorted(nums) lo = 0 hi = len(nums) - 1 while lo < hi : sum = nums_sorted[lo] + nums_sorted[hi] if sum < target: lo = lo + 1 elif sum > target: hi = hi - 1 elif sum == target: i = nums.index(nums_sorted[lo]) if nums.count(nums_sorted[hi]) > 1: j = nums.index(nums_sorted[hi], i+1) else: j = nums.index(nums_sorted[hi]) return i,j  Time complexity: $$O(n)$$ Space complexity: $$O(n)$$ Because this problem needs to return index, extra space is needed to store the sorted array. If the goal is to return numbers, the original space can be used to store them, so that the space complexity is $$O(1)$$.
2. 3Sum  Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. Notice that the solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
  Example 1: Input: nums = [-1,0,1,2,-1,-4] Output: [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]] Explanation: nums[0] + nums[1] + nums[2] = (-1) + 0 + 1 = 0. nums[1] + nums[2] + nums[4] = 0 + 1 + (-1) = 0. nums[0] + nums[3] + nums[4] = (-1) + 2 + (-1) = 0. The distinct triplets are [-1,0,1] and [-1,-1,2]. Notice that the order of the output and the order of the triplets does not matter.
 1） Brute Force Solution Three nested loops would exhaust all sums, which is obviously too complicated, so we drop this approach.
2） Converted to the TwoSum Problem with First Fixed Position def threeSum(self, nums: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]: nums = sorted(nums) res = [] target = 0 for i in range(0, len(nums)): if 0<i<len(nums)-1 and nums[i]==nums[i-1]: continue tuples=twoSum(nums[i+1:], target-nums[i]) for tuple in tuples: tuple.append(nums[i]) res.append(tuple) return res def twoSum(nums, target): lo = 0 hi = len(nums)-1 res = [] while( lo < hi ): left = nums[lo] right = nums[hi] sum = left + right if sum < target: while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left: lo = lo+1 elif sum > target: while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right: hi = hi-1 else: res.append([left, right]) while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left: lo = lo+1 while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right: hi = hi-1 return res  Time complexity: $$O(n^2)$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$
3. 4Sum Another similar problem on Leetcode is the 4Sum problem, and we can learn the idea of 3Sum to derive a solution.
4. k-Sum Problem: k-Sum Input:n integers (positive or negative) Decide: Are there k (distinct) numbers in the input which sum to Target integer?  The number of K can be any number greater than 2, as long as it does not exceed the size of the array.
Constraints：
 Whether the array is ordered or not？ Whether the elements in the array are distinct？ Whether the sub array is uniquely determined？  We use the generic approach, assuming that the array is unordered, that the array elements are duplicated and that the resulting subarray is not uniquely determined and may be 0 or more.
A generalized solution to the kSum problem using the idea of recursion. def nSum(nums, n, target): nums = sorted(nums) res = [] if n < 2 or len(nums) < 2: print("") elif n==2: lo = 0 hi = len(nums)-1 res = [] while( lo < hi ): left = nums[lo] right = nums[hi] sum = left + right if sum < target: while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left: lo = lo+1 elif sum > target: while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right: hi = hi-1 else: res.append([left, right]) while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left: lo = lo+1 while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right: hi = hi-1 return res else: for i in range(0, len(nums)): if 0<i<len(nums)-1 and nums[i]==nums[i-1]: continue tuples=nSum(nums[i+1:], n-1, target-nums[i]) if tuples != None: for tuple in tuples: tuple.append(nums[i]) res.append(tuple) return res  Time complexity: $$O(n^{k-1})$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$
summary: k-Sum Problem is like Input n integers (positive and negative) and Decide are there k numbers in the input that sum to target integer?

content:
Create slides in Markdown with Academic Academic | Documentation
 Features  Efficiently write slides in Markdown 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides Supports speaker notes Mobile friendly slides   Controls  Next: Right Arrow or Space Previous: Left Arrow Start: Home Finish: End Overview: Esc Speaker notes: S Fullscreen: F Zoom: Alt + Click PDF Export: E   Code Highlighting Inline code: variable
Code block:
porridge = "blueberry" if porridge == "blueberry": print("Eating...")   Math In-line math: $x + y = z$
Block math:
$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$
 Fragments Make content appear incrementally
{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}  Press Space to play!
One  Two  Three 
 A fragment can accept two optional parameters:
 class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS) weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears   Speaker Notes Add speaker notes to your presentation
{{% speaker_note %}} - Only the speaker can read these notes - Press `S` key to view {{% /speaker_note %}}  Press the S key to view the speaker notes!
 Only the speaker can read these notes Press S key to view    Themes  black: Black background, white text, blue links (default) white: White background, black text, blue links league: Gray background, white text, blue links beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links    night: Black background, thick white text, orange links serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links simple: White background, black text, blue links solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links   Custom Slide Customize the slide style and background
{{< slide background-image="/img/boards.jpg" >}} {{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}} {{< slide class="my-style" >}}   Custom CSS Example Let's make headers navy colored.
Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:
.reveal section h1, .reveal section h2, .reveal section h3 { color: navy; }   Questions? Ask
Documentation
